title: Fragments: All Things Are One
description: The surviving fragments of Heraclitus on the Logos, the unity of opposites, and the ever-living fire.
introduction: Heraclitus of Ephesus, known as 'the Obscure' even in antiquity, left behind a single work from which only fragments survive, preserved as quotations in later authors. His central insight — that all things are unified through the Logos, a rational principle governing perpetual change — profoundly influenced Stoic philosophy, early Christian theology (the Gospel of John opens with the Logos), and Western metaphysics.

translation.original_text: τοῦ δὲ λόγου τοῦδ᾿ ἐόντος ἀεὶ ἀξύνετοι γίνονται ἄνθρωποι καὶ πρόσθεν ἢ ἀκοῦσαι καὶ ἀκούσαντες τὸ πρῶτον.
translation.text: Though this Logos exists forever, people fail to comprehend it both before hearing it and after they have heard it for the first time.

translation.original_text: κόσμον τόνδε, τὸν αὐτὸν ἁπάντων, οὔτε τις θεῶν οὔτε ἀνθρώπων ἐποίησεν, ἀλλ᾿ ἦν ἀεὶ καὶ ἔστιν καὶ ἔσται πῦρ ἀείζωον, ἁπτόμενον μέτρα καὶ ἀποσβεννύμενον μέτρα.
translation.text: This cosmos, the same for all, no god nor man has made, but it always was and is and shall be: an ever-living fire, kindling in measures and going out in measures.

translation.original_text: οὐκ ἐμοῦ, ἀλλὰ τοῦ λόγου ἀκούσαντας ὁμολογεῖν σοφόν ἐστιν ἓν πάντα εἶναι.
translation.text: Listening not to me but to the Logos, it is wise to agree that all things are one.

translation.original_text: πόλεμος πάντων μὲν πατήρ ἐστι, πάντων δὲ βασιλεύς.
translation.text: War is the father of all and king of all; some he shows as gods, others as men; some he makes slaves, others free.

translation.original_text: ποταμοῖσι τοῖσιν αὐτοῖσιν ἐμβαίνουσιν ἕτερα καὶ ἕτερα ὕδατα ἐπιρρεῖ.
translation.text: Upon those who step into the same rivers, different and different waters flow.
